name: Starship vs Zhuque-3
description: A side-by-side comparison of SpaceX's Starship and LandSpace's Zhuque-3, two reusable liquid-oxygen/methane orbital rockets from the United States and China, covering scale, payload, and development status.
summary: Starship and Zhuque-3 are both reusable, liquid-oxygen/methane orbital rockets, but they are an order of magnitude apart in scale. Starship is the largest rocket ever built, aimed at crewed lunar and Mars missions; Zhuque-3 is a Chinese commercial medium-lift rocket aimed at competing with Falcon 9-class payloads.
keySimilarities: Both burn liquid oxygen and methane and target reusability.
Both are in active development rather than fully operational.
Both use methalox engines designed for eventual reuse.
keyDifferences: Starship is fully reusable and far larger; Zhuque-3 only recovers the first stage and is Falcon 9-class.
Starship targets crewed lunar/Mars missions; Zhuque-3 targets commercial satellite launch.
Raptor uses a full-flow staged-combustion cycle; TQ-12 uses a simpler gas-generator cycle.
useCases: Starship: very-large-payload and crewed deep-space missions, point-to-point and high-cadence orbital launch.
Zhuque-3: Chinese commercial and government medium-to-large satellites, with first-stage reuse to lower cost.
limitations: Starship payload figures are planned, not yet demonstrated on an orbital flight.
Zhuque-3 has not yet flown; performance numbers are manufacturer targets.
Direct comparison is asymmetric because the vehicles target different market segments.

faqs.question: Is Zhuque-3 China's Starship?
faqs.answer: No. Zhuque-3 is much smaller and targets Falcon 9-class commercial launch, while Starship is a fully reusable super-heavy vehicle for deep-space missions. They share methalox propulsion and reusability goals.

faqs.question: Which is closer to operation?
faqs.answer: Both are in development. Starship has conducted high-altitude and near-orbital integrated flight tests; Zhuque-3 has not yet conducted an orbital flight as of the last update.

faqs.question: Why compare them at all?
faqs.answer: They are two of the most prominent reusable methalox rockets in development in the US and China, which makes them useful benchmarks for each country's reusable-rocket progress.
